title: Cold tumbler on a digital balance
description: A covered glass tumbler of iced water sits on a digital balance. The student steps a five-minute timer forward again and again, watching droplets bead and merge on the tumbler's cold outer wall while the balance reading climbs on its own, with a floating '+mass' badge confirming every gain. Once the run finishes, the student reveals a mark showing the water level sealed inside the tumbler, seeing for themselves that it has not moved, before a full mechanism explainer ties the observation together and a 'What you have learned' finish screen closes out the activity.
context: Grade 6 Science, in the cluster of lessons on physical changes, condensation and the conservation of mass that follows the study of methods of separating substances. The tool renders, entirely on screen, the classic classroom demonstration of a cold drink or a covered glass of ice water 'sweating' on the outside: a phenomenon every student has seen on a humid day but rarely connects to a change in mass. It matters because it is the cleanest everyday encounter with the idea that matter is conserved and can move between states and locations — water vapour already present in the surrounding air changes state and deposits onto a cold surface, adding real, measurable mass to the setup, while the sealed liquid inside neither leaks nor evaporates away. It sits after condensation has been named and defined, using the tool to let the student watch the live, numeric read-out and the unchanged inside-level mark demonstrate the idea directly, rather than being asked to answer questions about it.
teachingNotes: Run this as a pure watch-and-explain observation, not a quiz: let the student work the timer at their own pace and watch the mass climb, with nothing to answer along the way. Pre-empt the specific misconception this tool targets: that a sealed, covered tumbler can only lose mass or hold steady, and that the droplets forming outside must be water that has 'seeped' or 'sweated' through the glass from the cold water inside. Use the toggleable inside-level mark as the decisive, undeniable piece of evidence — point out that the level does not drop even though the outside is now covered in droplets. The idea to land: condensation is water vapour from the surrounding air changing state onto a cold surface, and this adds mass to the whole setup even though the sealed contents are completely unchanged.
instructionsForStudent: 1) Read the setup: a covered glass of iced water is sitting on a digital balance. 2) Tap 'Step +5 min' repeatedly to advance the timer and watch the tumbler's outer wall and the digital reading. 3) Notice how droplets gather on the outside and the reading keeps climbing on its own. 4) Once the run finishes, tap 'Mark inside level' to reveal whether the sealed water level inside has changed. 5) Tap 'Explain it' to see the full mechanism behind what you just observed. 6) Tap 'Finish' to see a summary of what you've learned.

placement.where: After condensation has been introduced, the student steps the timer to watch droplets form and the reading climb, then reveals the inside-level mark as evidence before opening the explanation. In Teacher mode the agent demonstrates the same full run and hands the tool to the student; in Student mode the student drives the full run and the agent coaches with highlight() and reacts to reported events.

studentControls.effect: Advances the timer by one interval — droplets grow on the tumbler wall, ice shrinks, and the reading counts up.

studentControls.control: Tap 'Mark inside level' (after the run finishes)
studentControls.effect: Reveals the dashed inside-level line on the tumbler, showing it has not moved.
Opens the mechanism panel explaining where the extra mass came from.

studentControls.control: Tap the Teacher | Your turn toggle
studentControls.effect: Switches between the teacher's focused demo caption and the student run — both step through the same full set of timer intervals.
Mutes or unmutes the tap/step/finish sound cues.

studentControls.control: Tap 'Finish' (appears once the explain panel has been seen)
studentControls.effect: Ends the activity and opens the 'What you have learned' finish screen.

answerKey.note: This is a non-evaluating Observe tool: there is no correct id, choice, or scored action anywhere — it asks the student no questions. The student watches the timer run and reveals the inside-level evidence themselves, and the Finish screen reports what was explored, never a score or stars.

scientificFacts: Condensation is water vapour in the air changing back into liquid water when it touches a cold surface.
The droplets that form on the outside of a cold, covered tumbler add real mass to the whole setup — the balance reading genuinely goes up.
Glass is non-porous, so the water sealed inside a covered tumbler cannot leak or seep through it; the inside water level stays exactly the same.
Matter is conserved: the extra mass on the balance did not appear from nothing — it moved from water vapour already present in the surrounding air.

agentInstructions.description: <description>Shows a covered tumbler of iced water gaining mass on a digital balance as water vapour from the surrounding air condenses on its cold outer wall, while the sealed water level inside stays visibly unchanged.</description>
agentInstructions.teacherToolInstruction: <teacher_tool_instruction>ALWAYS call setOperatorMode("ai") before driving. ALWAYS call step() repeatedly through the full run and let the balance reading and droplets animate fully before speaking. ALWAYS call toggleLevel() once the run finishes to reveal the inside-level evidence, then call explain() to open the mechanism panel. ALWAYS use highlight(target) with ids from validIds.highlightTargets — most usefully "tumbler" or "balanceDisplay" — to draw the student's eye before you speak. NEVER call finish() in this mode and NEVER ask the student to tap the step button or any control — in this mode driving the tool is your job. ALWAYS ask a thinking question about what the student OBSERVED (what is forming on the glass, what the reading is doing) before advancing, and NEVER reveal the mechanism before the student has watched the droplets form and the reading rise.</teacher_tool_instruction>
agentInstructions.studentToolInstruction: <student_tool_instruction>ALWAYS watch the tumbler's outer wall closely as you step the timer: notice where the droplets are forming and what the digital reading is doing. ALWAYS tap 'Mark inside level' once the run finishes and check for yourself whether the sealed water level really moved. ALWAYS open 'Explain it' and see whether the mechanism matches what you expected. ALWAYS tap 'Finish' once you've seen the explanation.</student_tool_instruction>
agentInstructions.teacherRealInstruction: <teacher_real_instruction>A watch-and-explain digital-balance simulation for the 'condensation adds mass' beat in Condensation and Conservation of Mass. Use it in place of, or alongside, a live cold-glass demonstration so students see a numeric read-out confirm the idea and confront the specific misconception that the outside droplets 'seeped' from the sealed water inside — with no quiz questions, just direct observation and evidence.</teacher_real_instruction>
